cards.text:
Deepa's Growth
Deepa needs a new school uniform as she has grown taller and started a new school year.

Shopping for Cloth
Deepa's mother takes her to a shop and asks for a two-metre cloth piece for the new uniform.

Measuring Tools
The shopkeeper uses a metal measuring rod, while the tailor uses a flexible measuring tape.

Traditional Measurements
Deepa's mother instructs the tailor to increase the length by 'char angula' (four fingers width).

Discussion with Friends
Deepa shares her experiences, leading to a group discussion about measuring lengths traditionally.

Different Methods
  • Hardeep mentions measuring with an arm length.
  • Padma talks about farmers using strides.
  • Anish adds measuring with foot length.

Measuring with Body Parts
Tasneem suggests measuring the classroom table with a body part, and Deepa proposes using a handspan or 'balisht.'

Experimentation
The group measures the table using their handspans, noting different numbers for each of them.

Results
Variations arise because each person has a different handspan length.

Handspan Measurements
  • Anish: Slightly more than 13
  • Padma: 13
  • Tasneem: Slightly less than 13
  • Deepa: Between 13 and 14
  • Hardeep: 14

Conclusion Drawn
They realize the need for consistent measurement units as handspan varies from person to person.

Unit Example
Measurements result in numbers associated with a unit, like 13 handspans, signifying the number and unit.

Need for Standard Units
Consistency in measurement demands internationally recognized units, as body-based units differ.

Historical Context
In ancient India, units like angula, dhanusa, and yojana were used for various measurements.

Archaeological Discoveries
Scales with ruled markings were found in the Harappan Civilisation, hinting at ancient measurement systems.
The SI unit of length is metre. Its symbol is m.
One metre (m) is divided into 100 equal divisions. Each division is called a centimetre (cm).
1 cm length are further divided into 10 equal parts. The length of one of these smaller parts is called a millimetre (mm).
For measuring larger lengths, we use a larger unit called a kilometre (km) which is equal to 1000 metres. And for measuring smaller lengths, we use units such as centimetre or millimetre.

Evolution of Measurement Systems
Several systems of units evolved at different times in various parts of the world, leading to confusion as travel increased.

Standardization of Units
Countries adopted a set of standard units known as the International System of Units (SI units) to avoid confusion.

SI Unit of Length
The SI unit of length is the metre, symbolized by 'm.'

Understanding the Metre Scale
A metre scale is divided into 100 equal divisions, known as centimetres (cm).

The 15-cm Scale
  • The 15-cm scale marks from 0 to 15 facilitate easy measurement.
  • Each big mark denotes 1 cm.

Smaller Units and Millimetre
  • 1 cm is further divided into 10 parts.
  • Each small division is a millimetre (mm).
  • 1 mm = 0.1 cm

Larger Units and Kilometre
A kilometre (km) is a large unit used to measure longer distances, equivalent to 1000 metres.

Use of Smaller Units
For smaller lengths, centimetres (cm) and millimetres (mm) are commonly used.

Inch Scale Markings
  • Inch scale markings co-exist on some scales.
  • 1 inch equals 2.54 cm.

Units of the Past
Units like inch and foot were prevalent in earlier days and are still in use by some.

Pictures and Diagrams
Figures 5.2 and 5.3 illustrate the metre scale and 15-cm scale respectively, aiding visual understanding.

Flexibility of Measurement
Users can switch between centimetres, millimetres, inches, and feet based on necessity.

Practical Application
Understanding scale markings is essential for precise measurement in various fields.

International System Influence
Adoption of the SI units resolved international measurement discrepancies.

Everyday Measurement Tools
Scales and measuring tapes frequently display both SI units and traditional units to cater to diverse needs.

Appropriate Scales for Measurement
To measure the length of small objects like a pencil, use a 15-cm scale. For larger dimensions such as a room's height, utilize a metre scale or a measuring tape.

Flexible Tape for Curved Objects
A metre scale is not suitable for curved objects like tree girths. Use a flexible measuring tape, like a tailor’s tape, for such measurements.

Positioning the Scale During Measurement
Ensure the scale is placed in contact with the object along its length for accurate measurements.

Correct Eye Positioning
When measuring, position your eye directly above the point to ensure accurate alignment and reading.

Measuring with a Damaged Scale
If the scale's ends are damaged or zero is unclear, use from the nearest clear mark. Subtract the initial reading to determine the length.

Example of Calculation
For readings at 1.0 cm and 10.4 cm, the length is: 10.4 cm - 1.0 cm = 9.4 cm.

Sensory Aids for Measurement
Scales with raised markings are available to aid those who need to feel markings by touch.

Activity 5.1 - Let Us Measure
This activity involves practical measuring exercises using different scales and tape.

Units of Length Notation
Units are in lowercase: kilometre (km), metre (m), centimetre (cm), and millimetre (mm). At sentence beginnings, capitalize the unit.

Symbol Notation Rules
  • Symbols like km, m, cm and mm remain lowercase.
  • No 's' for plurals.
  • A full stop is used only at sentence ends.

Space Between Number and Unit
Always leave a space between the numeric value and its corresponding unit of measurement.

Common Size References
Generally, tailor’s tapes are used for measuring clothing sizes.

Zero Mark Visibility
Avoid using sections of the scale where the zero mark is not clearly visible.

Prohibition of Improper Use
Never initiate a measurement from a broken or faded mark if alternatives exist.

Practical Measuring Tips
  • Keep the scale flat and steady.
  • Ensure the object is aligned properly.
  • Avoid parallax errors by measuring at the proper eye level.

topics.topic: Measuring the length of a curved line

cards.text:
Lighting Strings on Verandah
Anish and his parents decorated their house verandah with electric string lights for a celebration.

Functionality of Electric String Lights
The electric string lights were hung along the arches, enhancing the aesthetic appeal of the celebration.

Measuring Curved Lines
For measuring curved surfaces, a flexible measuring tape can be employed to obtain accurate readings.

Alternative Measuring Techniques
Using a thread to trace along a curved line is an effective way to determine its length when no measuring tape is available.

Steps to Measure with a Thread
Bullet Points:
  • Stretch the thread along the curve.
  • Mark the spot at the end of the curve.
  • Straighten the thread carefully.
  • Use a metre scale to measure the straightened length.

Visual Aid: Fig. 5.8
Fig 5.8 demonstrates how a thread can be used for measurements when appended to a meter scale.

Practical Applications
These methods of measurement are especially useful in real-world applications where curves are predominant, like setting up decorations or construction.

Tools for Measuring Curved Surfaces
Numbered List:
  1. Flexible measuring tape
  2. Thread or string
  3. Metre scale for straightening & measuring
When distance is stated with respect to a fixed object or point, then this point is called a reference point.

Educational Visit Plan
One day, a teacher informs her students about an educational visit to a nearby garden.

Meeting Point
The students are instructed to reach the garden directly in the morning.

Distance Discussion
Deepa and her friends discuss whether the garden is closer or farther than their school.

Opinions on Distance
Tasneem and Padma believe the garden is closer. Meanwhile, Deepa and Anish think the school is closer.

Hardeep's Perspective
Hardeep feels both the garden and the school might be at an equal distance.

Reference Point
If everyone considered distances from a common point like the bus stand, observations would align.

Definition of Reference Point
A reference point is a fixed object or location used for measuring distances.

Kabaddi Court Preparation
Hardeep and friends are instructed by the sports teacher to draw lines for the Kabaddi court using limestone powder.

Requirement for Measuring Tape
Padma suggests retrieving a longer measuring tape from the sports room.

Establishing Reference Point for Court
Deepa proposes deciding on a point on the ground as a reference from which to measure distances to draw lines.

Padma's Trip to Delhi
Padma travels by bus eagerly to visit her grandparents in Delhi, observing kilometre stones as she goes.

Kilometre Stones
As Padma reads stones like 'Delhi 70 km,' she notes that they mark her distance from Delhi.

Approaching Destination
Each kilometre stone she passes indicates she is getting closer to her grandparents' house.

Kilometre Stone 70 km
A stone marked 'Delhi 70 km' means Padma is 70 km away from Delhi.

Kilometre Stone 60 km
As she sees 'Delhi 60 km,' her position has changed to being 60 km distant from Delhi.

Concept of Motion and Rest An object is in motion if its position changes with respect to a reference point over time. Conversely, it is at rest if its position remains unchanged with respect to the reference point.
Observation of Motion Deepa noticed that passengers in the bus remained in their seats over time, meaning they were at rest when the bus is the reference point.
Perception of Motion from Inside From inside the bus, passengers seemed at rest to Deepa because their positions did not change relative to the bus.
Perception of Motion from Outside From an external perspective, such as a building outside, both the passengers and the bus appeared to be in motion.
Importance of Reference Points Choosing a reference point is key in determining if an object is in motion or at rest. It determines your perception of motion.
Relative Motion The state of rest or motion is relative and depends on the observer’s point of view and reference point chosen.
Deepa's Observation Deepa observed both rest and motion simultaneously by considering different reference points: inside the bus and externally from the bus.
Defining Rest and Motion Rest: No position change with time relative to reference point. Motion: Position changes with time relative to reference point.
Example of Reference Point - **Reference Point Inside:** Bus - **Result:** Passengers at rest - **Reference Point Outside:** Building - **Result:** Passengers in motion
Conclusion on Motion The perception of whether someone is at rest or in motion can change based on the chosen reference point, highlighting the relative nature of these concepts.
When an object moves along a straight line, its motion is called linear motion.

Linear Motion
Objects moving along a straight line exhibit linear motion.

Example of Linear Motion
Identify instances of linear motion in your daily surroundings.

Circular Motion
Objects following a curved, circular path have circular motion.

Example of Circular Motion
Look for circular motion patterns around you, like a rotating fan blade.

Oscillatory Motion
Objects moving back and forth around a central position show oscillatory motion.

Example of Oscillatory Motion
A swinging pendulum exhibits oscillatory motion.

Periodic Motion
An object repeating its path after a regular time interval demonstrates periodic motion.

Circular and Oscillatory Motion
  • Both are types of periodic motion, as they return to their original state at regular intervals.

International System of Units (SI Units)
Standard units of measurement adopted globally are known as SI units.

SI Unit of Length
  • The meter (m) is the base unit for measuring length.

Conversions in Length
  • 1 kilometer (km) = 1000 meters (m)
  • 1 meter (m) = 100 centimeters (cm)
  • 1 centimeter (cm) = 10 millimeters (mm)

Reference Point
A fixed object or point used to describe the change in position of another object.

Definition of Motion
An object is in motion if it changes its position compared to a reference point over time.

Exploring Linear Motion
Observe and list movements in a straight line for better understanding.

Concept of Fixed Intervals
In periodic motion, objects return to initial positions after specific timeframes, much like clocks.
The International System of Units (SI units) has been adopted by countries as standard units of measurement.
An object is said to be in motion if its position changes with respect to a reference point with time.
